topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The stack of crumpled papers hit Leo’s desk with a soft, defeated thud. He stared at the mountain of homework, each page a testament to his mother’s peculiar organizational system. It was a haphazard collection of essays, math problems, and even a surprisingly detailed diagram of the water cycle. He ran a hand through his hair, the gesture tight and frustrated. "Seriously?" he muttered to the empty room, his voice tight. He picked up a worksheet for history, the ink slightly smudged, and felt a deep, simmering irritation bubble up in his chest. It wasn't the work itself that bothered him; it was the sheer, overwhelming volume, the evidence of an unspoken expectation he hadn't been prepared for. He sighed, a long, drawn-out sound, and began methodically separating the papers, his movements stiff with reluctant compliance.

[story 2]

"Are you *still* going to look at this?" Sarah asked, her tone laced with weary exasperation. She watched as Mark methodically sorted through the overflowing filing cabinet in the spare room. Every textbook, every graded quiz, every forgotten note was meticulously stored, labeled with faded index cards. Mark’s jaw was clenched, his shoulders hiked up toward his ears. He picked up a particularly dense calculus assignment, his fingers tracing the lines of the handwriting with a deliberate, almost aggressive scrutiny. A sharp exhale escaped him. He wasn't enjoying this task at all. He just wanted the chaos to stop. "It's just... everything," he grumbled, pushing a stray lock of hair back from his forehead. The sheer, relentless accumulation made his temples throb.

[story 3]

I found the box tucked away in the attic, and the sight of it made my stomach clench. It was a veritable archive of my academic life, neatly bound, color-coded, and utterly comprehensive. I leaned against a dusty trunk, crossing my arms tightly over my chest. My gaze swept over the sheer quantity of completed work—from kindergarten reports to advanced literature critiques. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat, a sound of pure vexation. Why? Why had she kept every single piece of paper? It felt like an indictment, a monument to a level of control I simply couldn't fathom. I picked up a stack of chemistry notes, the paper feeling brittle and strangely heavy in my hands. I needed to get out of here.
